title: Bobiverse vs Expeditionary Force: Head to Head Comparison
metaDescription: Dennis E. Taylor's Bobiverse vs Craig Alanson's Expeditionary Forceâ€”two fun space opera series compared. Which humorous sci-fi adventure is better?

book1.title: We Are Legion (We Are Bob)
book1.subtitle: Bobiverse, Book 1
book1.author: Dennis E. Taylor
book1.description: We Are Legion (We Are Bob) is the first book in the Bobiverse series, a delightfully fun take on space exploration. Bob Johanson, a software engineer, wakes up a century after his death to find he's been uploaded into a computer and turned into a von Neumann probe. His mission: explore the galaxy and replicate himself. But Bob isn't alone for longâ€”each copy develops its own personality, leading to a universe full of Bobs with different quirks, missions, and pop culture obsessions. The series blends hard science fiction concepts with humor, heart, and philosophical questions about identity and consciousness.

book2.title: Columbus Day
book2.subtitle: Expeditionary Force, Book 1
book2.author: Craig Alanson
book2.description: Columbus Day kicks off the Expeditionary Force series with Earth caught in the middle of an intergalactic war. When aliens invade, Joe Bishop and his unit are thrown into a conflict they don't understand. After a devastating battle, Joe finds himself aboard an alien starship with Skippy, a sarcastic AI beer can who might be the most powerful intelligence in the galaxy. The series combines military sci-fi action with comedy, as Joe and Skippy become humanity's unlikely champions against galaxy-spanning threats. It's a wild ride full of humor, creative problem-solving, and genuine heart.

table.book2: Human soldier with alien AI sidekick

book1Strengths: The concept is brilliantly originalâ€”watching Bob multiply and develop different personalities is endlessly entertaining
Hard science fiction that respects physics while remaining accessible and fun
Each Bob clone feels like a distinct character with their own quirks and goals
The Star Trek references and pop culture humor hit perfectly for geeks
Philosophical depth about identity, consciousness, and what makes us human
The audio narration by Ray Porter is absolutely phenomenalâ€”might be the best in sci-fi
book1Weaknesses: The action can feel slow compared to military sci-fiâ€”more thinking than fighting
If you don't enjoy pop culture references, the humor might fall flat
Some readers find the self-replication concept gets confusing with too many Bobs
Later books in the series become more complex and less focused than book one
book2Strengths: Skippy the Magnificent is one of the funniest characters in science fictionâ€”period
Fast-paced military action keeps you turning pages from start to finish
The underdog humanity story is incredibly satisfyingâ€”Joe outsmarts advanced aliens
Great balance of humor and genuine stakesâ€”you laugh but still care
The series keeps going strong with 15+ books if you fall in love with it
R.C. Bray's audiobook narration brings Skippy to life perfectly
book2Weaknesses: The science is much softerâ€”relies on 'magic' tech that might frustrate hard sci-fi fans
Skippy's sarcasm can become repetitive if his humor doesn't click with you
Military focus means lots of tactical descriptions that may bore non-military readers
The long series means quality varies, and some books feel like filler

book1: I'm not dead. I'm also not exactly alive. I'm not sure what I am, except confused.
If I'm going to be a computer, I might as well be a space probe.
The worst thing about being replicated is that all your copies know your password.
We're exploring strange new worlds. We're seeking out new life. We're boldly going where no Bob has gone before.
Being immortal doesn't mean you stop caring. It means you have time to care about more things.
I died. I woke up. Then things got weird.
book2: I am Skippy the Magnificent, and you will address me as such, monkey.
Humans are like duct tapeâ€”not pretty, but we'll hold anything together in an emergency.
The galaxy is a dangerous place. Fortunately, I'm dangerous too.
You can't fix stupid, but you can numb it with copious amounts of alcohol.
I've been alone for millions of years. Now I'm stuck with you idiots.
Hope is not a strategy, Joe. But sometimes, it's all we've got.

book1: You love hard science fiction that explores realistic space travel and physics
You want clever problem-solving and exploration rather than constant action
You're a Star Trek fan or enjoy pop culture references woven into the story
You're fascinated by questions of identity, consciousness, and AI
You want a complete series (5 books) that wraps up satisfyingly
book2: You want fast-paced military action with humor that keeps you laughing
You love underdog stories where humans punch above their weight class
You want a long series (15+ books) to binge if you fall in love with it
You prefer character-driven comedy over hard science accuracy
You want audiobooks with exceptional narration—R.C. Bray and Skippy are legendary

verdict.summary: According to us, these are both fantastic space opera series, but they scratch different itches. Bobiverse is smarter and more thoughtful—hard science fiction with heart and clever problem-solving. If you want to think while you laugh, Bob is your guy. Expeditionary Force is funnier and more action-packed—it's military sci-fi with a sarcastic AI that will make you laugh out loud. In our opinion, Bobiverse is the better-written series with more depth, but Expeditionary Force is more entertaining and addictive.
verdict.recommendation: Read Bobiverse if you want hard sci-fi that respects physics, love exploration and problem-solving, or enjoy philosophical questions about consciousness. The Ray Porter audiobooks are mandatory—they elevate the series to another level. Bob is clever, heartfelt, and genuinely original. Read Expeditionary Force if you want action-packed military sci-fi with laugh-out-loud humor. Skippy is worth the price of admission alone. The series is longer (15+ books), so you're signing up for a major commitment. Honestly, read both. Start with Bobiverse if you prefer smart sci-fi, or Expeditionary Force if you want pure fun. Both are best experienced as audiobooks—the narrators make these series legendary.
